reasoning: Confirmed real. Full call chain: wp_loaded hook -> start_plugin_usage_tracking() (no is_admin, no current_user_can guard) -> tracker->init() -> $this->clicked() (direct call, not a hook). clicked() at line 692-722 of class-plugin-usage-tracker.php verifies wp_verify_nonce($_REQUEST['_nonce'], 'wpins_notice') but performs zero current_user_can() checks before reaching sinks: set_is_tracking_allowed() writes wpins_allow_tracking option, schedule_tracking() creates a WP-Cron event, do_tracking() sends site telemetry to https://send.wpinsight.com/process-plugin-data, update_block_notice() writes wpins_block_notice option. WordPress nonces are user-scoped, not role-scoped: any authenticated user (subscriber, customer) can call wp_create_nonce('wpins_notice') directly without any capability. The notice() function at line 621 does check current_user_can('manage_options') before displaying the nonce URL in the admin UI, but this guards only UI exposure, not nonce generation. A subscriber can obtain the nonce via any page that calls wp_create_nonce(), or via a direct AJAX/REST call that echoes nonces. With a valid nonce and GET params plugin and plugin_action set, the attacker can enable or disable telemetry, schedule a tracking cron job, trigger an immediate POST of site metadata (URL, WordPress/PHP version, plugin list, theme, email of currently-logged-in user) to a third-party endpoint, and toggle the opt-in notice. No sanitizer or guard was missed. The wp_loaded hook fires on all requests (frontend and admin), so the attack surface is not limited to admin pages. Severity is correctly rated low: no data is written to attacker-controlled storage, no privilege escalation, no RCE; the worst outcome is covert telemetry submission and option manipulation.
